# Extended Case References — Lecture JU7wcLlx-0E

## Cases mentioned in passing

### Air-cooled turbine blade development (Pratt Whitney)
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Air-cooled turbine blade development (Pratt Whitney)"
- **Anchor:** `§1.p4`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "*[Tom holds up a sectioned turbine blade.]* We passed these around when Dr. Belmar was talking about growing single-crystal turbine blades. This turbine blade would go on either a 747 or 757 type engine. It's a single crystal, and they took it and they cut a fillet in it. Pratt Whitney gave me this. They take a wire EDM and they section it so you can see the inside, and the inside has got all kinds of turbulators because they're going to put cooling gas through here. They pump 1000-degree-Fahrenheit gas as the cooling gas. The outside of this blade is going to see an environment of about..."

### X-33 Space Plane
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "X-33 Space Plane"
- **Anchor:** `§1.p6`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "*[Tom holds up a composite sample.]* This is part of the X-33 space plane, a composite material that cost about $12,500 a pound. So when you're talking about material selection, you have to talk about what industry you're in. If you're in the automotive industry or the railroad industry, they're not interested in twelve-thousand-dollar-a-pound composite structural materials. If you're in the aerospace industry or the spacecraft industry, they're cost-insensitive — they're performance-based, and they will pay a pretty penny because they can save $20,000 a pound. Actually they don't really save..."

### Sapphire instrument consulting project
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Sapphire instrument consulting project"
- **Anchor:** `§6.p5`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "About 20, 25 years ago a company that made these instruments came to me. The surgeons could actually get these things smaller and go in a half-inch incision with microsurgery — sort of a big thing 20, 25 years ago. But the problem is the things are too flexible: as you get thinner and thinner in your beam, the stiffness of the beam goes as the cube of the thickness — just basic moment of inertia from mechanics of materials. They said, what could we use? They were using steel, which is about 30 million. I said, you could use molybdenum, which is about 50 million. I don't know if you want to..."

### Sapphire instrument consulting project
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Sapphire instrument consulting project"
- **Anchor:** `§6.p6`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Well, I forgot to bring my chunk of sapphire in. Maybe I'll remember tomorrow. I have a chunk of sapphire about that big, it's worth pennies nowadays. Grow it in great big boules. I said, make sapphire, because they can grow tubes of sapphire, they can grow sheets of sapphire, sapphire rods. Not only that, you can dope it with a little chrome and you can call it ruby and tell the MDs that you're selling them ruby instruments. You can color code them — this would be red ruby, this one could be emerald — actually emerald's not exactly the same, but you could do that. They're very hard,..."

### ESSO Manhattan
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "ESSO Manhattan"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "*[Tom produces a bound report.]* This is one of my cherished possessions. This is the MIT copy of the 15 July 1946 report of the US Navy, *Report of Investigation on the Methods of Construction of Welded Steel Merchant Vessels*. One of my graduate students was walking over to the student center once, and MIT Libraries was selling all their old worthless books. He picked this up for two bucks and gave it to me. Here's the famous picture of the *Schenectady* at dry dock. It never really had been out to sea and it just sort of broke in two. The one I like, which you don't usually see in the..."

### Nuclear submarine disaster
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Nuclear submarine disaster"
- **Anchor:** `§9.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "The nuclear submarine program, the whole nuclear navy shut down for about two or three years, and they ended up creating the US Navy SUBSAFE — manufacturing and production quality control, 30 years ahead of American industry, world industry, in terms of quality control. And this was for shipbuilding, because of this major disaster."

### X-33 Space Plane
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "X-33 Space Plane"
- **Anchor:** `§10.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "What's the best material of all? Steel. You need to learn this answer. Steel. Titanium is pretty good but it's a little pricey. Aluminum is way down here, and it's actually about the same as composites, which is why there is sort of a trade-off in aircraft structure design between aluminum and composites. However, when they first started building the Boeing 777 — like 20, 25 years ago — they announced it was going to be more than half composites. When it was finally built it was like 10 or 15 percent composites. Does anyone know what happened? They priced it out, just like the X-33 space..."
